mission.title: Install and Configure Cadence Workflow on Kubernetes
mission.description: Cadence is a distributed, scalable, durable, and highly available orchestration engine designed to execute asynchronous long-running business logic. Installing Cadence allows you to build and run scalable workflows efficiently, making it ideal for complex business processes.

steps.title: Add the Cadence Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Cadence Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add cadence https://cadence-workflow.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Cadence using Helm
steps.description:
Install Cadence in a new namespace called 'cadence'. This command will deploy the Cadence server and its dependencies.
```bash
helm install cadence cadence/cadence --namespace cadence --create-namespace --version v1.4.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all Cadence pods are running successfully in the 'cadence' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace cadence
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Cadence deployment to set resource limits for the Cadence server pods. This step ensures that your Cadence instance has the necessary resources allocated.
```bash
kubectl patch deployment cadence --namespace cadence --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources", "value": {"limits": {"cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi"}, "requests": {"cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi"}}}]'
```

steps.title: Access the Cadence Web UI
steps.description:
Port forward the Cadence Web UI service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/cadence-web --namespace cadence 8088:8088
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have Cadence running with all pods in the 'cadence' namespace in a 'Running' state. You can access the Cadence Web UI at http://localhost:8088 to manage and monitor your workflows.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add cadence https://cadence-workflow.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install cadence cadence/cadence --namespace cadence --create-namespace --version v1.4.0

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Cadence Helm release to remove the deployment and its associated resources from the cluster.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'cadence' namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current state of your Cadence deployment to ensure you can restore it if needed.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to apply the latest changes.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that all Cadence pods are running successfully after the upgrade to ensure the process completed without issues.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when a pod fails to start repeatedly. Check the pod logs to diagnose the problem using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace cadence
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Cadence Web UI, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. You can check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace cadence
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the Cadence pods are not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource limits and requests set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment cadence --namespace cadence
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check the error message for clues. You can also check the current Helm release status with:
```bash
helm status cadence --namespace cadence
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
